title: Pollo al Ajillo
subtitle: Spanish garlic chicken with dry sherry
excerpt: Pollo al ajillo is Spanish chicken braised with a large amount of garlic, dry sherry, and olive oil. The garlic mellows and sweetens as it cooks. The sauce is the sherry and chicken juices combined, concentrated into something glossy and aromatic. Ready in under 40 minutes.
sourceNotes: Pollo al ajillo is one of the most widely cooked chicken dishes in Spain. The dish appears in different forms across regions but the core of lots of garlic, olive oil, and sherry is consistent. The version here uses bone-in thighs, which give the richest result.

content.text: Pollo al ajillo is Spanish chicken braised in a pan with a generous amount of garlic cloves, dry sherry, and olive oil. The garlic is cooked whole or halved and mellows from sharp and pungent to sweet and nutty in the process. The sherry and chicken juices reduce into a glossy, fragrant sauce.

Pat the chicken thighs dry. Season generously with salt and pepper. Heat {{olive-oil}} in a large casserole or wide pan over medium-high heat. Brown the chicken, skin-side down, for 6 to 7 minutes until the skin is deep golden. Turn and brown the other side for 3 minutes. Transfer to a plate.
Reduce the heat to medium. Add the whole garlic cloves to the pan. Cook in the chicken fat for 3 minutes, turning, until lightly golden on all sides.
Pour in {{sherry-dry}} of dry sherry. Let it bubble for 2 minutes. Add {{chicken-stock}} of chicken stock, rosemary, and bay leaves. Return the chicken to the pan, skin-side up. Bring to a simmer.

Reduce the heat to low. Cover and cook for 20 minutes until the chicken is cooked through (the juices run clear when pierced at the thickest point). Remove the lid, increase the heat to medium-high, and cook for 5 minutes more until the sauce reduces to a glaze. Remove the bay leaves and rosemary. Scatter with parsley.

items.symptom: The sauce is thin and pale
items.cause: Sherry not reduced enough or not enough initial browning
items.fix: Brown the chicken properly before adding the sherry; the fond (stuck bits) in the pan contributes colour and flavour to the sauce. Reduce the sauce uncovered at the end.

items.symptom: The garlic is still sharp after cooking
items.cause: Garlic added too late or not cooked in the fat long enough
items.fix: The garlic needs at least 3 minutes in the hot fat before the sherry goes in. The whole garlic cloves should feel soft when pressed before serving.

content.text: Pollo al ajillo is one of the most beloved weeknight chicken dishes in Spain. The combination of good chicken, generous garlic, and dry sherry is as close to a formula as Spanish cooking gets. Serve with crusty bread to mop the sauce and a simple salad on the side.
